title: Never assume unstated deliverables are understood
overview: Explains why all expected deliverables must be explicitly represented in the WBS.
detailed_summary: A recommended WBS guideline is to never assume stakeholders will naturally know a deliverable element is part of the project if it is not included in the WBS. If a deliverable is omitted from the WBS, it may be omitted from subsequent planning and may be overlooked until late in the project.
content: The WBS should explicitly list deliverables rather than relying on stakeholder assumptions or implied understanding. Unstated items are vulnerable to being forgotten during planning, scheduling, and budgeting. Explicit representation is especially important for items that may seem obvious to one stakeholder but not to others. The WBS serves as the visible agreement on scope, so anything expected should be shown.
relationships: Explicit WBS representation reduces omission risk
search_queries: Should all deliverables be explicitly listed in the WBS?
Can stakeholders assume omitted deliverables are included?
Why not rely on unstated assumptions in a WBS?
common_questions: Why do omitted deliverables cause problems?
Should obvious items still be in the WBS?
How explicit should a WBS be?
can_answer: Why assumptions are risky in WBS creation
How explicit the WBS should be
examples: If the Minister deliverable is left off a wedding service WBS, planning for engaging the minister may be missed until late.
best_practices: Review the WBS for items stakeholders believe are 'understood' but not shown.
warnings: Omitted deliverables can cause late schedule and budget changes.
limitations: Explicit listing does not replace later activity definition, but it prevents scope invisibility.
troubleshooting: If a forgotten item emerges late, check whether it was omitted from the WBS and update related plans accordingly.
decision_rules: If a deliverable matters to project scope, include it explicitly in the WBS even if it seems obvious.
